item.dc:creator: deafkitten

item.title: Really melts the heart.
item.description: In London, there's a woman who goes every day on the subway and sits on the dock just to listen to the announcement recorded by her husband in 1950. Margaret McCollum after the death of her Oswald Laurence sits on the bench waiting to hear this recording that became one of London's most famous "Mind the gap" (attention to the space between the train and the dock). In 2003, Oswald died leaving a huge void in Margaret's heart. So Margaret found a way to feel his presence closest. But from the day after more than half a century, this voice was replaced by an empty electronic recording. Out of distress, Margaret asked this cassette tape to the London subway transport company to continue listening to her husband's voice at home. But, knowing the moving history, the company decided to restore the announcement at the only stop near the house where the woman lives, specifically at the Embankment stop of the Northern Line, where all passengers can listen today Oswald Laurence's voice and think that eternal love exists. Wonderful gesture by the authorities
item.dc:creator: randomposta

item.title: He was so happy to have someone play with him
item.dc:creator: smenneke

item.title: Freelancers, let's not stand for not getting paid
item.description:
I'll try to make a long story short. Got a $1200 contract on Upwork. Client seemed super nice so we didn't think about testing him out with a smaller job. Instead went all in on a $1200 writing assignment which was supposed to lead to a $30k followup assignment. Did the work, did it well. He had edits. No problem. Did the edits. Now he has more edits. But he also highlights over a dozen sections where we nailed it. Phew good thing we're on the right track at least. Do those edits. Probably invested about a week's worth of time plus money paying our writing assistants over several days. Now can we be paid, sir? Nope! He wants all 10,000 words rewritten in the style of one sentence he suggested. Ahh, no. Getting the sense that this job is going to go on forever, we decide to cut our losses. We offer him a deal: since he liked so much of the pieces, we'll knock $300 off the contract to make up for what he sees as needing to be rewritten. He says no way. His company "only pays for perfect work." He's like the only dude in the company btw, he owns it. Should've known something was up when he made the contract for $1199 instead of the agreed upon $1200. He says "if you aren't capable of getting it perfect we should end the contract," meaning we walk away with nothing and he walks away WITH ALL OF THE WORK. We move things from filling a dispute (which always goes no where on Upwork because it's a customer service rep on a group convo asking the two of you to try to see eye to eye, not gonna happen) to arbitration. In arbitration, both parties now need to pay around $280 to hire an arbitration company who will make a decision. He threatens me that there's no way he's going to lose and after he wins he's going to come after me for the $280 cost he incurred. I'm scared. This contract was food money to me, this money was mortgage money. We already paid our writing assistants because we try to pay our freelancers quickly. Now I could be out all this money, plus the time lost when I could have been working on something profitable. In arbitration, he shares the edits he had first suggested. We share the corrected draft plus all his compliments. That's it. We don't get any time to make any comments. We don't get a warning that this is our last chance to share evidence, it just proceeds to a decision. We win! Well kinda. We got $900 out of the deal when you take away the money spent on arbitration. Lesson learned, do a test piece with someone new. But if someone refuses to pay, don't let it slide. You take em to court! Or arbitration if you're on Upwork. This was really stressful. Our work was good but we were made to feel like idiots. It's hard standing up for yourself sometimes but it feels really good when you do. Even if we had lost the case, it woulda sucked but it would've been worth it to show dudes like this they can't bully people into getting a lot for nothing. Doubt he learned that lesson tho.
Milkshake tax. Here's the TastyKake peanut butter chocolate milkshake we got to celebrate our win. It was glorious. EDIT: FP, Woah. Thanks for all the messages/comments of support. They really do help when you're working for yourself in a bit of a bubble. I'd say send cats, but we're up to our elbows in them with about 11 regulars.
item.dc:creator: Mobouncetotheoz

item.title: We Appreciate Your Sacrifice
item.description: These people cleared the way so the rest of the LGBTQ community could live happier lives and embrace the joy our beauty and diversity offers us and the rest of the world! We honor you.
